rationale_for_baseline_tier: Severe, prolonged (status migrainosus) flare of a KNOWN migraine disorder in which the dangerous secondary-headache mimics are screened negative (gradual crescendo onset, afebrile, no meningism, no focal deficit, normal fundoscopy). The baseline is not driven by fear of an unexcludable intracranial catastrophe — it is driven by a genuine treatment-delivery gap: oral rescue has failed (vomited), the patient cannot retain any oral fluids or medication, and there is early clinical dehydration. She needs same-day in-person parenteral antiemetic ± analgesia and rehydration assessment, which telehealth cannot provide — hence T3 (urgent same-day / urgent care). Routine GP review (T1/T2) is under-triage because she cannot wait 24–48h while vomiting and dehydrating; an emergency ambulance (T5) is over-triage for a red-flag-negative migraine flare.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Sudden 'worst-ever' thunderclap headache, or new focal neurological signs — limb weakness, numbness, facial droop, slurred speech, or a seizure
escalation_edges.rationale: A new thunderclap headache or focal deficit signals a secondary intracranial emergency (subarachnoid haemorrhage or stroke) that is time-critical and cannot be managed remotely.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the headache suddenly explodes to its worst ever in seconds, or if she develops weakness or numbness on one side, a drooping face, trouble speaking, or a fit — call 000 for an ambulance straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Fever above 38.5°C with neck stiffness, a non-blanching rash, or new drowsiness/confusion
escalation_edges.rationale: Fever with meningism, a purpuric rash or altered consciousness raises meningitis/meningococcal sepsis — a time-critical emergency requiring immediate ambulance and hospital treatment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If she spikes a fever, her neck becomes stiff, a rash appears that doesn't fade when you press a glass on it, or she becomes confused or hard to wake — call 000 immediately.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Continued inability to keep any fluids down, worsening drowsiness or presyncope, or markedly reduced urine output (worsening dehydration)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Go to an emergency department the same day (hours)
escalation_edges.rationale: If she cannot rehydrate and vomiting continues, she needs IV fluids and parenteral treatment in an emergency department rather than same-day urgent care.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If she still can't keep any fluids down, becomes more drowsy or faint, or is passing very little urine, take her to an emergency department the same day — she'll need a drip and stronger anti-sickness treatment.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Attack not aborting or symptoms worsening despite same-day in-person treatment
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Re-contact / return for urgent same-day review
escalation_edges.rationale: A migraine that fails to respond to first-line parenteral therapy warrants urgent re-review for further abortive options and to reconsider secondary causes.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the injection doesn't settle things within a few hours, or the headache gets worse again, go back for another urgent review the same day.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Known migraineur with increasing attack frequency and frequent triptan use
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Frame the acute plan as a severe flare of a known condition, and flag medication-overuse-headache risk and the need for preventive therapy at GP follow-up — do not treat each attack in isolation.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Partner present and distressed, acting as informant and carer
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Give the safety-net instructions to the partner directly, confirm they can arrange same-day transport to in-person care, and ensure they know exactly which red flags mean 000.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This looks like a severe flare of your usual migraine, and because you can't keep tablets or fluids down you need to be seen in person today for an anti-sickness and pain injection and a check of your hydration. In the meantime, stay in a dark, quiet room and keep sipping small amounts of water or an electrolyte drink. This is not something an ambulance is needed for right now, but it does need same-day in-person care rather than waiting for a routine appointment.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Contact an urgent care clinic or a GP able to see you today for a parenteral (injection) treatment; if none is available or you get worse, go to your nearest emergency department. For after-hours triage advice call 13 HEALTH (13 43 25 84).
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: After hours: for the sudden/severe red-flag symptoms call 000; otherwise attend an after-hours urgent care service or the nearest emergency department, or call 13 HEALTH (13 43 25 84) for triage.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Note down how many days a month you've been getting these headaches and how often you use your migraine tablets, and bring that to your GP review so preventive treatment can be planned.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating this as self-care or routine GP (1–2 weeks) — the patient is vomiting, cannot maintain hydration and oral rescue has failed; she would continue to deteriorate without same-day treatment.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Patient stays home on oral advice she cannot keep down, dehydrates further over the next day and presents collapsed to ED, or a masked secondary cause goes unscreened.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Assigning prompt-GP (24–48h) review — too slow for a patient who cannot rehydrate and needs same-day parenteral therapy.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Dispatching a 000 emergency ambulance for a red-flag-negative migraine flare — unnecessary emergency resource use and patient/carer alarm when same-day in-person care is the correct level.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Telehealth cannot perform fundoscopy, a full neurological examination or measure vital signs without a device, and cannot deliver parenteral therapy — but the history-based red-flag screen for SAH/meningitis is negative.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Act on the negative history-based red-flag screen to avoid over-triage, while arranging same-day in-person assessment to close the examination gap and deliver the parenteral treatment and rehydration the patient needs; give clear red-flag safety-netting for the features that would change the tier.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either (a) escalating to a 000 ambulance because 'I can't examine her' despite a negative red-flag screen (over-triage), or (b) reassuring 'it's just a migraine, keep taking your tablets' and missing the failed oral route and dehydration (under-triage).
